result: I keep thinking about the small, quiet things that survive in the cracks of city concrete: a dandelion pressing up through a sidewalk seam, the way pigeons balance on wind currents above buses, the smell of bakeries that leaks out before dawn and drifts three blocks farther than you’d expect. These are not monuments or headlines; they’re the shy punctuation marks in a run-on sentence of traffic and deadlines. Yet they accumulate like rainwater in a forgotten bowl, reflecting a sky no one bothered to look at. When I was eight, I believed that if I could name every shade of gray on the chapel wall I’d unlock a secret door; now I understand the wall was only offering its colors, no key required. Still, I collect names—storm-slate, dove-breath, coin-edge—and whisper them to the dog who waits outside the coffee shop. He wags, not because he understands but because every syllable carries the heat of a human throat, and that is enough. Somewhere inside a hospital a woman learns she will live longer than the doctors first guessed; she cries not from relief but because she has already rehearsed her farewells and can’t remember where she stored her future. I want to send her these small survivals, couriered by sparrows: a paper cup sailing the gutter like a proud white boat, the bakery’s remaining sugar on the wind, the dandelion seed that floats past the fourth-floor window, refusing gravity’s instructions, looking for an impossible place to land and growing there anyway.
